title: US Sits on 100+ Approved Chinese Blacklist Entries as AI Export Controls Become Bargaining Chips
subhead: The US government approved DeepSeek and over 100 Chinese firms for the Entity List but never published them — holding them as leverage in trade talks. Meanwhile, OpenAI released GPT-5.6 after government review, the UK banned social media for under-16s, and an IISS report detailed how Russian shadow fleet drones mapped NATO nuclear bases with near-impunity.

sections.heading: US Approved DeepSeek Blacklist Entry, Then Sat on It for Eight Months
sections.body:
An interagency committee cleared DeepSeek, CXMT, and more than 100 other Chinese firms for addition to the US Entity List — but the Commerce Department has published none of them. The gap now exceeds eight months, the longest since the list became a serious policy tool, according to Reuters.

Commerce Under Secretary Jeffrey Kessler has avoided new listings since late 2025, reportedly for fear of escalation. The blacklist has become a bargaining chip: China controls rare earth supplies that US defense, auto, and chip firms cannot replace quickly, giving Beijing leverage at the negotiating table. As Yahoo Finance reports, trade policy has effectively absorbed national security policy.

The cost is tangible. Some unlisted firms allegedly supplied components for Russian drones recovered in Poland, while others sold restricted Nvidia chips into Chinese universities. Anthropic and OpenAI both told US officials that DeepSeek attempted to extract capabilities from their models. Meanwhile, Commerce promised to replace a Biden-era rule governing global access to US AI chips but never did, and is not enforcing the old one — creating a loophole that may already be moving chips to Chinese firms operating outside China.
sections.whyItMatters: Export controls are now flexing with the trade cycle, not the threat cycle. The Entity List is less predictable and less feared — and Beijing can read the pause as confirmation that pressure on rare earths buys breathing room on chips.

sections.heading: OpenAI Releases GPT-5.6 After Government Review; Anthropic Fable/Mythos Restored
sections.body:
OpenAI publicly released GPT-5.6 Sol, Terra, and Luna on July 9, roughly two weeks after limiting rollout to a small group of trusted partners at the US government's request. CEO Sam Altman posted 'Happy building' on X to mark the launch. OpenAI also announced GPT-Live voice models that can listen and speak simultaneously, rolling out globally to ChatGPT users.

The release follows Anthropic's restoration of Claude Fable 5 and Mythos 5 after the US Department of Commerce lifted export control directives in late June. Both cases stem from the Trump administration's June AI executive order, which asks model developers to provide cutting-edge models to the government for capability assessment ahead of full release, and gave agencies 60 days to develop an evaluation framework.

OpenAI stated it does not believe 'this kind of government access process should become the long-term default,' adding that it 'keeps the best tools from users, developers, enterprises, cyber defenders, and global partners who need them.' The company said it is working with the government to develop a 'repeatable process for future model releases.'
sections.whyItMatters: The US has established a de facto pre-release review process for frontier AI models — voluntary in name, but effectively mandatory for the top labs. The question now is whether this becomes normalized or remains a contested exception.

sections.heading: Russian Shadow Fleet Drones Mapped NATO Nuclear Sites With Near-Impunity
sections.body:
An International Institute for Strategic Studies (IISS) report documents 144 drone incidents across 13 European states between August 2024 and February 2026, with roughly 48% over military facilities and 26% over critical infrastructure like ports and energy sites. Germany alone recorded 58 sightings.

The pattern was most pronounced around nuclear deterrence infrastructure. Drones were spotted over Volkel Air Base in the Netherlands — which hosts US B61-12 gravity bombs under NATO sharing arrangements — on at least three separate days in November and December 2025. Base security fired ground-based weapons but the drones evaded direct hits and no wreckage was recovered. Kleine-Brogel Air Base in Belgium and the French ballistic missile submarine base at Ile Longue were also surveilled.

The IISS assesses it is highly likely the Kremlin conducted the campaign, with drones launched from Russian shadow fleet vessels operating near European coastlines. French naval commandos boarded one suspect tanker, the Boracay, on September 28, 2025, and found two employees of a Russian private military company on board. The only publicly attributed incident came on February 26, 2026, when the Swedish military jammed a drone launched from the Russian signals intelligence ship Zhigulevsk toward the French aircraft carrier Charles de Gaulle during a port visit to Malmo.

The report concludes that Europe's counter-drone architecture does not yet match the threat, with uneven detection, fragmented legal authorities, and attribution too slow to support deterrence. NATO Secretary-General Mark Rutte has stated the alliance would not shoot down all drones as a matter of policy. The 'Drone Wall' initiative aims for initial operational capability by end of 2026, but no mandate exists to engage the vessels that launch them.
sections.whyItMatters: Russia has demonstrated it can probe NATO's most sensitive military sites at low cost and high deniability, while Europe's response remains fragmented. The drone campaign's primary enabling mechanism — shadow fleet launch platforms — remains intact.

sections.heading: UK Bans Social Media for Under-16s, Sets Chatbot Age Floor at 18
sections.body:
UK Prime Minister Keir Starmer announced a sweeping ban on social media access for children under 16, covering platforms including TikTok, Snapchat, Instagram, X, and YouTube. The ban, expected to take effect in spring 2027, also raises the minimum age for chatbots that imitate romantic interactions to 18. Messaging services like WhatsApp and Signal are exempt.

The government will also ban livestreaming features and stranger-to-child contact for under-16s across all platforms, and is considering an overnight social media curfew for under-18s, with details expected in July. Starmer called it 'a line in the sand,' saying 'tech giants had their chance and failed.'

According to WIRED, the ban follows a public consultation that received over 100,000 submissions, with more than 90% of participating parents supporting an outright ban. The move mirrors Australia's under-16 social media ban, which took effect in July 2026. Platform responses were critical: YouTube called it a push toward 'less safe services,' while Snap argued that 'an outright ban that disconnects teens from those relationships doesn't make them safer.'

A former Starmer adviser told WIRED the ban was rushed through to shore up parliamentary support amid leadership challenge concerns.
sections.whyItMatters: The UK has become the largest democracy to implement a blanket social media ban for minors, setting a precedent that other EU and Commonwealth nations may follow — and testing whether platform-level enforcement is even possible at scale.

sections.heading: Zhipu AI Raises $4 Billion in Hong Kong, Eyes Custom Chips as GLM-52 Demand Surges
sections.body:
Chinese AI company Zhipu AI (listed as Knowledge Atlas Tech Joint Stock, HK:2513) raised HK$31.37 billion (US$4 billion) through a discounted share sale in Hong Kong, pricing 19.78 million shares at HK$1,588 each — a nearly 13% discount from the prior close. The company's shares have surged nearly 1,300% since its IPO in January 2026, fueled by demand for its GLM series of AI models.

According to The Information, Zhipu is also exploring the development of a custom AI chip (ASIC) for its GLM model family, approaching several Chinese chip designers. Daily token usage for its latest model, GLM-5.2, has grown 27x. The Atlantic reports that GLM-5.2 is being called 'a step change' — with venture capitalist Marc Andreessen calling it 'the first Chinese AI model to match and often beat the top public US models,' and Vercel CEO Guillermo Rauch saying he is 'genuinely impressed, almost shocked' by its coding abilities.

On the open-source platform Hugging Face, Chinese models accounted for nearly half of all downloads from February 2025 to 2026. On OpenRouter, the six most popular AI models are Chinese, with GLM-5.2 already ranking fifth in under a month. Coinbase claims to have nearly cut its AI spending in half by defaulting to cheaper models including GLM-5.2 and Kimi.
sections.whyItMatters: Zhipu's $4B raise and custom chip ambitions signal that Chinese AI labs are building vertically integrated stacks — from models to silicon — to sidestep US export controls. The price gap between Chinese and American models is reshaping enterprise adoption patterns.

sections.heading: Russia Builds Arctic Infrastructure for 80,000 Troops as NATO Stands Up Finland Force
sections.body:
A joint investigation by Nordic and Baltic media outlets found that Russia is constructing new military facilities along its borders with Norway and Finland designed to accommodate tens of thousands of troops. Finnish Army Chief Pasi Valimaki told the investigation: 'We expect to have 80,000 soldiers on our border, and that can be compared to the fact that we previously had 20,000.' A separate Finnish report found Russia expanding a base at Novaya Vilga, roughly 100 miles east of the Finnish border, to hold up to 6,000 troops.

The investigation identified satellite imagery showing 'new barracks for thousands of soldiers, long lines of military vehicles and ammunition storage' built throughout the winter. Sweden's military intelligence chief, Thomas Nilsson, called the buildup 'a threat that we should take seriously.' A NATO official told The War Zone that while the alliance assesses near-term conflict risk as low, 'Moscow could seek to expand westward into the Nordic and Baltic nations after a ceasefire with Ukraine.'

On June 6, NATO stood up its Forward Land Forces (FLF) Finland, with a Swedish-led battalion battlegroup based in Boden, Sweden, and a multinational staff element in Rovaniemi, Finland, under SACEUR command. NATO also launched Operation Arctic Sentry in February 2026 to strengthen deterrence across the region.
sections.whyItMatters: Russia is building the infrastructure for a post-Ukraine military posture on NATO's northern flank — not deploying 80,000 troops now, but preparing the capacity to do so. NATO's response force, while real, remains a battalion-level presence against what could become a corps-sized threat.

sections.heading: G7 Sets 60% Cap on Chinese Rare Earth Dependency, But Price Floor Falls Apart
sections.body:
At the G7 summit in Evian-les-Bains, France, leaders endorsed a measurable supply chain resilience target for the first time: reducing dependence on any single non-G7 supplier of rare earths and permanent magnets to below 60% by 2030, with a longer-term objective of 50%. China currently controls 93% of permanent magnet production and an estimated 85-91% of global rare earth refining, per a CSIS analysis.

However, the G7 failed to agree on a coordinated price floor mechanism for critical minerals. The central dispute was over who should bear the cost: governments were reluctant to use public funds, while industry pushed back against passing costs to end users in price-sensitive sectors like automotive. The result is a target with enforcement teeth but no economic engine to build the alternative supply chains.

Meanwhile, President Trump threatened France with 100% tariffs on wine and champagne unless it scraps its 3% digital services tax on US tech companies including Amazon, Meta, and Alphabet. The threat, delivered ahead of the summit, overshadows the rare earths cooperation and highlights the tension between G7 unity on strategic minerals and bilateral trade disputes over digital taxation.
sections.whyItMatters: The 60% dependency cap is the first quantified G7 commitment on critical minerals — but without a price floor or coordinated procurement, the target is aspirational. Japan spent 15 years trying to de-risk rare earth supply chains and remains the world's largest importer of Chinese rare earth metals. Targets don't build refineries.

bottomLine: The US is holding export control designations for DeepSeek and 100+ Chinese firms as trade leverage rather than security enforcement — a choice that weakens the Entity List as a deterrent. OpenAI and Anthropic have both emerged from government-imposed model restrictions, but the precedent of pre-release review is now established. In Europe, the IISS report on Russian shadow fleet drones mapping NATO nuclear bases reveals a threat operating below the threshold of allied response. Zhipu's $4B raise and custom chip push shows Chinese AI labs building full-stack alternatives to US-controlled infrastructure, while the G7's first quantified rare earth target lacks the economic mechanisms to achieve it.
sources: Reuters, Yahoo Finance, CNBC, The War Zone, IISS, Stars and Stripes, WIRED, AP, BBC, The Atlantic, The Information, EconoTimes, Bloomberg, Channel News Asia, CSIS, Meduza
